wikipedia_title: Numerical methods for ordinary differential equations
display_title: Numerical methods for ordinary differential equations

annotations.label: Initial value problem (first-order ODE)

anchor.snippet: A first-order differential equation is an Initial value problem

annotations.note: Mathlib expresses the IVP relation via integral curves (`HasDerivAt γ (v t (γ t))`) plus an initial value, but has no single named 'initial value problem' definition.

annotations.label: First-order equation

anchor.snippet: First-order means that only the first derivative of y appears in the equation

annotations.note: This is descriptive terminology, not a formalized statement, though Mathlib does work exclusively with first-order systems via `HasDerivAt`.

annotations.label: Reducing a second-order ODE to first-order system

anchor.snippet: the second-order equation y ′′ = − y can be rewritten as two first-order equations

annotations.note: Mathlib has no general order-reduction construction for higher-order ODEs.

annotations.label: Picard–Lindelöf theorem

anchor.snippet: The Picard–Lindelöf theorem states that there is a unique solution

annotations.note: Local existence is given by `exists_eq_forall_mem_Icc_hasDerivWithinAt` and uniqueness by `ODE_solution_unique` in `Mathlib.Analysis.ODE.Gronwall`.

annotations.label: Euler method

anchor.snippet: This is the Euler method

annotations.note: Mathlib has no Euler method or other numerical ODE integrators.

annotations.label: Euler method as an explicit method

anchor.snippet: The Euler method is an example of an explicit method

annotations.note: No notion of explicit numerical methods exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Backward Euler method

anchor.snippet: we get the backward Euler method

annotations.note: Mathlib has no backward Euler scheme.

annotations.label: Backward Euler is implicit

anchor.snippet: The backward Euler method is an implicit method

annotations.note: No notion of implicit numerical methods exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponential integrators

anchor.snippet: Exponential integrators describe a large class of integrators

annotations.note: Mathlib has no exponential integrator methods.

annotations.label: First-order exponential integrator

anchor.snippet: The first-order exponential integrator can be realized by holding

annotations.note: No exponential integrator scheme is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multistep method

anchor.snippet: This yields a so-called multistep method

annotations.note: Mathlib has no multistep numerical methods.

annotations.label: Leapfrog method

anchor.snippet: the leapfrog method which is second order

annotations.note: The leapfrog method is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Linear multistep methods

anchor.snippet: Almost all practical multistep methods fall within the family of linear multistep methods

annotations.note: Linear multistep methods are not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Almost all practical multistep methods fall within the family of linear multistep methods

annotations.label: Runge–Kutta methods

anchor.snippet: This leads to the family of Runge–Kutta methods

annotations.note: Runge–Kutta methods are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Variable step-size methods

anchor.snippet: variable step-size methods have been developed

annotations.note: No adaptive step-size machinery exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Error indicator

anchor.snippet: the methods must also compute an error indicator

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of a numerical error indicator.

annotations.label: Variable order method

anchor.snippet: this is called a variable order method

annotations.note: Variable order methods are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multiderivative methods

anchor.snippet: multiderivative methods , which use not only the function f but also its derivatives

annotations.note: Multiderivative numerical methods are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Nyström methods

anchor.snippet: Nyström methods work directly with second-order equations

annotations.note: Nyström methods are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Geometric integration methods

anchor.snippet: are especially designed for special classes of ODEs

annotations.note: Geometric/structure-preserving integrators are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantized state systems methods

anchor.snippet: Quantized state systems methods are a family of ODE integration methods

annotations.note: Quantized state system methods are not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parallel-in-time methods

anchor.snippet: Parallel-in-time (PinT) methods have been developed in response to these issues

annotations.note: Parallel-in-time integration methods are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Convergent numerical method

anchor.snippet: A numerical method is said to be convergent if the numerical solution approaches the exact solution

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of convergence of a numerical scheme.

annotations.label: Listed methods are convergent

anchor.snippet: All the methods mentioned above are convergent.

annotations.note: No convergence results for numerical ODE methods are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Local (truncation) error

anchor.snippet: The local (truncation) error of the method is the error committed by one step of the method

annotations.note: Local truncation error is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Consistent method

anchor.snippet: The method is said to be consistent if

annotations.note: Consistency of a numerical method is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The method is said to be consistent if

annotations.label: Order of a method

anchor.snippet: The method has order

annotations.note: The order of a numerical method is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Consistency from positive order

anchor.snippet: Hence a method is consistent if it has an order greater than 0.

annotations.note: This consistency-from-order implication is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Hence a method is consistent if it has an order greater than 0.

annotations.label: Convergence requires consistency and zero-stability

anchor.snippet: for a method to be convergent, it must be both consistent and zero-stable

annotations.note: The Dahlquist equivalence theorem (convergence ⇔ consistency + zero-stability) is not in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: for a method to be convergent, it must be both consistent and zero-stable

annotations.label: Euler methods have order 1

anchor.snippet: introduced above both have order 1, so they are consistent

annotations.note: No order analysis of the Euler methods exists in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: introduced above both have order 1, so they are consistent

annotations.label: Global (truncation) error

anchor.snippet: A related concept is the global (truncation) error

annotations.note: Global truncation error is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Global error of one-step method implies convergence

anchor.snippet: in particular, such a method is convergent

annotations.note: This convergence result for one-step methods is not formalized in Mathlib, though `discrete_gronwall` provides related discrete error-bound infrastructure.

annotations.label: Stiffness

anchor.snippet: is described as stiffness

annotations.note: Stiffness of ODEs is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite Difference Method for BVPs

anchor.section: Numerical solutions to second-order one-dimensional boundary value problems
anchor.snippet: is called the Finite Difference Method

annotations.note: The finite difference method for boundary value problems is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Central difference approximations are not formalized in Mathlib (only `slope` for exact divided differences exists).

anchor.section: Numerical solutions to second-order one-dimensional boundary value problems
anchor.snippet: the second-order central difference for the second derivative is given by

annotations.note: The central difference formula for the second derivative is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.note: Discretization of a boundary value problem into a linear system is not formalized in Mathlib.
